title: Sandesh

ingredients.title: Ingredients
items: Lemon juice or vinegar – 2 tbsp (for curdling)
Sugar – ¼ to ⅓ cup (adjust to taste)
Saffron strands – a few (optional, for aroma & color)
Rose water or kewra water – ½ tsp (optional)
Chopped pistachios or almonds – 1 tbsp (for garnish)

instruction.title: Step 1: Prepare Fresh Chhena (Paneer)
steps: Boil the milk in a heavy-bottomed pan on medium heat. Stir occasionally to prevent sticking.
When the milk comes to a gentle boil, turn off the heat.
Add lemon juice or vinegar slowly while stirring gently until the milk curdles. You’ll see whey separating from the curds.
Let it sit for 5 minutes. Then, strain through a muslin/cheese cloth.
Rinse the curds under cold water to remove any sourness.
Hang the cloth for 30 minutes to drain excess water. The resulting soft chhena is ready.

instruction.title: Step 2: Knead the Chhena
steps: Transfer the chhena to a clean plate.
Knead it for 8–10 minutes with the heel of your palm until smooth and slightly glossy. (This step is crucial for silky Sandesh.)

instruction.title: Step 3: Sweeten and Flavor
steps: Transfer the kneaded chhena to a non-stick pan on low heat.
Add sugar gradually while stirring continuously.
Cook gently for 5–7 minutes until sugar dissolves completely and the mixture comes together. Do not overcook, or it will dry out.
Add cardamom powder, saffron strands, and rose/kewra water. Mix well.

instruction.title: Step 4: Shape the Sandesh
steps: Remove from heat and let it cool slightly (warm, not hot).
Grease your hands lightly with ghee or water. Take small portions and shape them into flat discs, balls, or traditional molded shapes.
Press gently and garnish with chopped nuts or saffron strands.

instruction.title: Step 5: Serve
steps: Sandesh is best served fresh at room temperature or slightly chilled.
Can be stored in an airtight container in the fridge for 2–3 days.

chefTips: Use full-fat milk for the richest flavor.
Do not overcook the chhena with sugar; it should remain soft and melt-in-mouth.
For extra finesse, some chefs fold in a little khoya/mawa for richness.
Experiment with flavors: pistachio, mango, chocolate, or saffron for a gourmet touch.
